annotations.label: Arithmetic mean

anchor.snippet: the sum of a collection of numbers divided by the count of numbers in the collection

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.average` for a finite counting measure equals (∑ f i)/n; `Finset.centerMass` with uniform weights also expresses this.

annotations.label: Arithmetic mean formula

anchor.snippet: the arithmetic mean is defined by the formula

annotations.note: `Finset.centerMass` with weights w ≡ 1 is exactly (∑ z_i)/n, recovering the standard arithmetic-mean formula.

annotations.label: Mean of monthly salaries

anchor.snippet: if the monthly salaries of

annotations.note: Numerical illustration of the mean formula; no such example in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Population mean and sample mean

anchor.snippet: the mean of that population is called the population mean

annotations.note: Mathlib does not distinguish 'population mean' vs 'sample mean' statistical terminology; only the abstract average is present.

annotations.label: Centroid (mean of vectors)

anchor.snippet: can be similarly defined for vectors in multiple dimensions

annotations.note: `Finset.centerMass` is defined for vectors in any module, covering the centroid as a special case with uniform weights.

annotations.label: Residuals sum to zero

anchor.snippet: the numbers to the left of the mean are balanced by the numbers to the right

annotations.note: No direct lemma `∑ (x_i − mean) = 0` was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Translational invariance of the mean

anchor.snippet: the mean is translationally invariant

annotations.note: No statement of `mean(x + c) = mean(x) + c` found, though `integral_add_const` provides related infrastructure.

annotations.label: Mean minimizes sum of squared deviations

anchor.snippet: it minimizes the sum of squared deviations from the typical value

annotations.note: The least-squares characterization of the mean does not appear to be in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sample mean as best predictor

anchor.snippet: The sample mean is also the best single predictor because it has the lowest root mean squared error

annotations.note: No RMSE-minimization statement for the sample mean is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sample mean is unbiased estimator

anchor.snippet: the estimate of it that is unbiased is the arithmetic mean of a sample drawn from the population

annotations.note: Unbiasedness of the sample mean estimator is not formalized; no statistics framework with 'unbiased estimator' was found.

annotations.label: Scale independence (first-order homogeneity)

anchor.snippet: The arithmetic mean is independent of scale of the units of measurement

annotations.note: `Finset.centerMass_smul` shows the center of mass is homogeneous in the points, giving scale linearity of the mean.

annotations.label: Mean lies between extremes

anchor.snippet: always between the largest and smallest values in that sample

annotations.note: `Finset.centerMass_le_sup` (with its dual `inf_le_centerMass`) bounds the weighted mean by the max/min values.

annotations.label: Mean of means of equal-sized groups

anchor.snippet: is the arithmetic mean of the arithmetic means of each group

annotations.note: `Finset.centerMass_segment'` combines center-of-mass values over disjoint index families, generalizing pooling of equal-sized group means.

annotations.label: Mean equals median for arithmetic progression

anchor.snippet: When the values in a data set form an arithmetic progression, the median and arithmetic mean are equal

annotations.note: No median-vs-mean lemma for arithmetic progressions was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mean vs median differ

anchor.snippet: In other cases, the mean and median can differ significantly

annotations.note: Illustrative example not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Weighted average

anchor.snippet: an average in which some data points count more heavily than others

annotations.note: `Finset.centerMass t w z = (∑ w i • z i) / ∑ w i` is precisely the weighted average.

annotations.label: Mean of a function

anchor.snippet: the mean of a function is loosely defined as the average value of the function over its domain

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.average μ f = ⨍ x, f x ∂μ` is the integral average of a function over its domain.

annotations.label: Mean of a probability distribution

anchor.snippet: is called the mean of the probability distribution

annotations.note: For a probability measure, `MeasureTheory.average μ id` equals the integral ∫ x ∂μ — the distribution's mean.

annotations.label: Mean, median, mode equal for normal distribution

anchor.snippet: all measures of its central tendency, including not just the mean but also the median mentioned above and the mode

annotations.note: `ProbabilityTheory.gaussianReal` exists but no lemma equating its mean, median, and mode is present.

annotations.label: Arithmetic mean of angles fails

anchor.snippet: Taking the arithmetic mean of 1

annotations.note: No formalization of the failure of naive averaging on angles.

annotations.label: Mean as central point with modular distance

anchor.snippet: define the mean as the central point

annotations.note: No circular/angular mean definition was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bar notation for arithmetic mean

anchor.snippet: often denoted by a bar

annotations.note: Mathlib uses `⨍` notation for `MeasureTheory.average`, not the over-bar convention from statistics.
